skill.name: react-email
skill.description: Build React Email templates for transactional and marketing messages, including styling, rendering, localization, and sending guidance.
skill.summary: Create production-ready HTML email templates with React Email components.
skill.author: resend

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical combinations of command execution, network use, and credential access, but review found these are mostly documentation examples for React Email workflows. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration was found. The skill still warrants a medium warning because it instructs agents to run package-manager commands, send email through providers, read environment secrets, and use dynamic locale imports in sample code.

medium_findings.title: Agent May Run Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to scaffold projects, install dependencies, and run a local development server. These commands are legitimate for React Email setup, but npx create-email@latest and install commands execute third-party package code and should require normal user confirmation in restricted environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit setup instructions in the main skill file. The risk is real but contextual because the commands support the advertised development workflow rather than hidden behavior.

medium_findings.title: Email Sending Uses Environment Secrets
medium_findings.description: Sending examples use API keys and SMTP credentials from environment variables and perform outbound email delivery through Resend, Nodemailer, and SendGrid. This is expected for an email skill, but misuse could expose credentials or send unintended email if an agent acts without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples directly instantiate email providers with process.env secrets and send messages. The behavior is intentional and documented, so this is a capability risk rather than malicious credential access.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Locale Imports Should Validate Input
medium_findings.description: Internationalization examples build import paths from a locale value. If copied into an application without restricting locale to an allowlist, this pattern can cause unsafe module resolution or build-time path exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present and uses interpolated locale values. Exploitability depends on how applications source and validate locale, so confidence is high for the risk pattern but not for active harm.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Flagged Markdown and JSX as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are false positives caused by markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and JSX template literals in documentation. Review found no hidden Ruby or shell backtick execution in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown fences or static documentation examples. They do not execute when the skill is loaded.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Example or Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to React Email documentation, Resend documentation, example.com placeholders, local preview guidance, and sample email links. No evidence found that the skill silently contacts these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in documentation links and sample template data. I found no code path that performs unsolicited network requests from the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Based Blocker Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptographic algorithm, system reconnaissance, network reconnaissance, and C2 keyword alerts match ordinary email template text, Tailwind breakpoint names, test labels, and setup documentation. No evidence found of malicious reconnaissance, cryptography abuse, or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner labels are driven by token matches such as md, reset, and test identifiers. The surrounding context is documentation and validation criteria, not malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unpinned CLI Execution in Setup Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide uses latest package-manager commands to scaffold a React Email project. This is normal for modern JavaScript tooling but executes code from the package registry.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package execution commands are explicit and user-facing. The concern is supply-chain exposure, not hidden malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Import Path Built from Locale
dangerous_patterns.description: Sample i18n code interpolates locale into relative import paths. Applications should restrict locale values to known language codes before using this pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is present in multiple examples. The practical impact depends on application bundler behavior and locale validation.

content.value_statement: HTML email development is difficult because email clients support different markup and CSS. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through React Email templates, styling rules, previews, localization, and safe sending patterns.
actual_capabilities: Creates React Email templates for welcome, reset, notification, order, and newsletter messages.
Applies React Email components such as Html, Body, Container, Button, Img, Tailwind, and Preview.
Guides setup of the React Email preview server and project directory structure.
Explains email-client styling limits for images, layout, media queries, borders, and Tailwind utilities.
Provides rendering and sending examples for Resend, Nodemailer, and SendGrid.
Covers localization patterns with next-intl, react-i18next, and react-intl.
limitations: It cannot guarantee identical rendering across all email clients without external client testing.
It depends on user-provided brand assets, verified sending domains, and production asset URLs.
It includes examples that may run package-manager commands or send email when used by an agent.
Locale import examples need application-specific validation before production use.

use_cases.title: Transactional Email Buildout
use_cases.description: Create typed React Email templates for onboarding, password reset, verification, and account notifications.

use_cases.title: Email Design System Migration
use_cases.description: Convert existing HTML emails into reusable React components with consistent spacing, typography, and buttons.

use_cases.title: Localized Email Launch
use_cases.description: Structure templates and message files for multi-language campaigns using supported React i18n libraries.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single email
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React Email welcome template for my product. Ask for brand color, logo, tone, and production asset URL before writing the template.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Email
prompt_templates.scenario: Review and refine current template code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my React Email template for email-client compatibility. Fix unsupported CSS, improve layout, and keep the existing copy intact.

prompt_templates.title: Add Sending Support
prompt_templates.scenario: Connect a rendered email to an email provider
prompt_templates.prompt: Add safe sending guidance for this React Email template using Resend. Use environment variables, verified domains, and explicit error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Localized Email System
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a localized React Email setup for English, Spanish, and French. Include message organization, locale validation, preview props, and fallback behavior.

output_examples.input: Create a password reset email for a SaaS app.
output_examples.output: A typed React Email template with preview text, branded button, reset link props, expiry copy, fallback text, and client-safe styling guidance.

output_examples.input: Make this order confirmation email work better in Outlook and Gmail.
output_examples.output: A revised layout using React Email rows, columns, inline-safe styles, explicit borders, fixed image rules, and notes about remaining client limits.

output_examples.input: Add Spanish and French support to my welcome email.
output_examples.output: A localization plan with message keys, language files, validated locale choices, fallback text, and preview examples for each supported language.

best_practices: Ask for brand colors, logo format, tone, and asset hosting before creating a template.
Use email-safe layouts, absolute image URLs, alt text, typed props, and plain-text rendering.
Require user confirmation before running package commands, using API keys, or sending email.
anti_patterns: Do not use SVG, WEBP, flexbox, grid, media queries, or dark-mode selectors in email templates.
Do not hardcode localhost or private asset paths in production email content.
Do not send test or production email without verified domains, reviewed recipients, and explicit approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build React Email templates for transactional and marketing messages using email-safe components and styling patterns.

faq.question: Can it send emails?
faq.answer: It provides sending examples for Resend, Nodemailer, and SendGrid. Actual sending requires user credentials and approval.

faq.question: Does it support Tailwind CSS?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends the React Email Tailwind component and pixel-based presets for email-client compatibility.

faq.question: Can it handle localization?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents patterns for next-intl, react-i18next, and react-intl, with message files and fallback behavior.

faq.question: What assets work best in email?
faq.answer: Use hosted PNG or JPEG images with absolute URLs and alt text. Avoid SVG and WEBP for broad client support.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes legitimate commands, outbound email examples, environment secrets, and dynamic imports. These require normal user review.
